interpretations.interpretation: The audience likely understood “the travail of his soul” as the suffering and hardships endured by a righteous servant figure, after which he will be rewarded with satisfaction and vindication—suggesting that endurance through suffering leads to eventual relief and approval from God.

Many listeners could have taken “by his knowledge” as meaning that the servant’s understanding of God’s will—his teaching, faithfulness, or obedience—is what leads others to be put in the right. “Justify” would be heard as being set right/recognized as righteous before God, not merely “declared innocent” in a legal technical sense.

The phrase “justify … the many” may have pointed the audience toward a deliverance-centered hope: the servant’s faithful role is seen as beneficial for a large group (the nation or a broader community). This would fit expectations that Israel’s God acts to restore many people.

“He shall bear their iniquities” could have been understood as vicarious burden-bearing: the servant takes on the consequences of wrongdoing (in some tangible or experiential way), so that the community’s moral/spiritual condition changes. For contemporary hearers, this could imply that suffering is connected to cleansing/atonement.

Some audience members might have read “the servant” less as an individual Messiah and more as a representative person or group (e.g., the faithful within Israel). In that reading, the suffering and bearing of iniquities would be interpreted as the faithful portion absorbing the effects of the community’s sins.

Others could have interpreted the servant as a specific righteous leader or prophet who embodies God’s message. “Bearing iniquities” would then be heard as the prophet/leader enduring blame, persecution, or divine judgment that falls on the people, while remaining faithful.

The overall movement from suffering (“travail”) to results (“shall be satisfied,” “justify many”) would likely have been heard as a reversal of present disgrace: the righteous servant’s current low status is not final, and God’s action will overturn it.
